title: Ecclesiastical province

infobox.value: is one of the basic forms of [jurisdiction](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_jurisdiction "Ecclesiastical jurisdiction") in [Christian churches](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"), including those of both [Western Christianity](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity") and [Eastern Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity"), that have traditional hierarchical structures. An ecclesiastical province consists of several [dioceses](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese") (or [eparchies](/wiki/Eparchy "Eparchy")), one of them being the [archdiocese](/wiki/Archdiocese "Archdiocese") (or [archeparchy](/wiki/Archeparchy "Archeparchy")), headed by a [metropolitan bishop](/wiki/Metropolitan_bishop "Metropolitan bishop") or [archbishop](/wiki/Archbishop "Archbishop") who has [ecclesiastical jurisdiction](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_jurisdiction "Ecclesiastical jurisdiction") over all other bishops of the province.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Type of territorial division within Christian churches

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article includes a list of [general references](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#General_references "Wikipedia:Citing sources"), but **it lacks sufficient corresponding [inline citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Inline_citations "Wikipedia:Citing sources")**. Please help to [improve](/wiki/Wikipedia:WikiProject_Reliability "Wikipedia:WikiProject Reliability") this article by [introducing](/wiki/Wikipedia:When_to_cite "Wikipedia:When to cite") more precise citations. *(April 2012)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

An **ecclesiastical province** is one of the basic forms of [jurisdiction](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_jurisdiction "Ecclesiastical jurisdiction") in [Christian churches](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"), including those of both [Western Christianity](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity") and [Eastern Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity"), that have traditional hierarchical structures. An ecclesiastical province consists of several [dioceses](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese") (or [eparchies](/wiki/Eparchy "Eparchy")), one of them being the [archdiocese](/wiki/Archdiocese "Archdiocese") (or [archeparchy](/wiki/Archeparchy "Archeparchy")), headed by a [metropolitan bishop](/wiki/Metropolitan_bishop "Metropolitan bishop") or [archbishop](/wiki/Archbishop "Archbishop") who has [ecclesiastical jurisdiction](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_jurisdiction "Ecclesiastical jurisdiction") over all other bishops of the province.

In the [Greco-Roman world](/wiki/Greco-Roman_world "Greco-Roman world"), *ecclesia* ([Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): ἐκκλησία; [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *ecclesia*) was used to refer to a lawful assembly, or a called [legislative body](/wiki/Legislative_body "Legislative body"). As early as [Pythagoras](/wiki/Pythagoras "Pythagoras"), the word took on the additional meaning of a community with shared beliefs.[[1]](#cite_note-1) This is the meaning taken in the Greek translation of the [Hebrew Scriptures](/wiki/Hebrew_Scriptures "Hebrew Scriptures") (the [Septuagint](/wiki/Septuagint "Septuagint")), and later adopted by the Christian community to refer to the assembly of believers.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

In the history of [Western world](/wiki/Western_world "Western world") (sometimes more precisely as [Greco-Roman world](/wiki/Greco-Roman_world "Greco-Roman world")) adopted by the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") and the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire"), Christian ecclesiastical provinces were named by analogy with the secular [Roman province](/wiki/Roman_province "Roman province") as well as certain [extraterritorial](/wiki/Extraterritoriality "Extraterritoriality") formations of the western world in early medieval times (see [Early Middle Ages](/wiki/Early_Middle_Ages "Early Middle Ages")). The administrative seat of each province is an [episcopal see](/wiki/Episcopal_see "Episcopal see"). In hierarchical Christian churches that have dioceses, a *province* is a collection of those dioceses (as a basic unit of administration).

Over the years certain provinces adopted the status of [metropolis](/wiki/Metropolis_(religious_jurisdiction) "Metropolis (religious jurisdiction)") and have a certain degree of self-rule. A bishop of such province is called the *metropolitan bishop* or *metropolitan*. The [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") (both Latin and Eastern Catholic), the [Orthodox](/wiki/Orthodoxy "Orthodoxy") Churches and the [Anglican Communion](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion") all have provinces. These provinces are led by a *metropolitan archbishop*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ecclesiastical_province&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early history")]

Ecclesiastical provinces first corresponded to the civil provinces of the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"). From the second half of the 2nd century, the bishops of these provinces were accustomed to assemble on important occasions for common counsel in [synods](/wiki/Synod "Synod"). From the end of that century the summons to attend these increasingly important synods was usually issued by the bishop of the capital or metropolis of the province, who also presided over the assembly, especially in the East. Important communications were also forwarded to the bishop of the provincial capital to be brought to the notice of the other bishops. Thus in the East during the 3rd century the bishop of the provincial metropolis came gradually to occupy a certain superior position, and received the name of metropolitan.[[3]](#cite_note-Catholic-3)

At the [First Council of Nicaea](/wiki/First_Council_of_Nicaea "First Council of Nicaea") (325) this position of the metropolitan was taken for granted, and was made the basis for conceding to him definite rights over the other bishops and [dioceses](/wiki/Dioceses "Dioceses") of the state province. In Eastern canon law since the 4th century (cf. also the [Synod of Antioch](/wiki/Synod_of_Antioch "Synod of Antioch") of 341, can. ix), it was a principle that every civil province was likewise a church province under the supreme direction of the metropolitan, i.e. of the bishop of the provincial capital.[[3]](#cite_note-Catholic-3)

This division into ecclesiastical provinces did not develop so early in the Western Empire. In North Africa the first metropolitan appears during the 4th century, the [Bishop of Carthage](/wiki/Bishop_of_Carthage "Bishop of Carthage") being recognized as primate of the dioceses of Northern Africa; metropolitans of the separate provinces gradually appear, although the boundaries of these provinces did not coincide with the divisions of the empire. A similar development was witnessed in [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"), [Gaul](/wiki/Gaul "Gaul"), and [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"). The migration of the nations, however, prevented an equally stable formation of ecclesiastical provinces in the Christian West as in the East. It was only after the 5th century that such gradually developed, mostly in accordance with the ancient divisions of the Roman Empire. In Italy alone, on account of the central ecclesiastical position of Rome, this development was slower. However, at the end of antiquity the existence of church provinces as the basis of ecclesiastical administration was fairly universal in the West. In the [Carolingian period](/wiki/Carolingian_period "Carolingian period") they were reorganized, and have retained their place ever since.[[3]](#cite_note-Catholic-3)

## Provincial church organisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ecclesiastical_province&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Provincial church organisation")]

### Catholic Church

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ecclesiastical_province&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Catholic Church")]

Further information: [List of Catholic dioceses (alphabetical)](/wiki/List_of_Catholic_dioceses_(alphabetical) "List of Catholic dioceses (alphabetical)")

#### In general

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ecclesiastical_province&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: In general")]

In the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), a province consists of a metropolitan archdiocese and one or more (1–13) [suffragan dioceses](/wiki/Suffragan_diocese "Suffragan diocese") headed by diocesan bishops or territorial prelatures, apostolic vicariates and missions sui iuris. The [archbishop](/wiki/Archbishop "Archbishop") of the metropolitan see is the [metropolitan](/wiki/Metropolitan_bishop#Roman_Catholic "Metropolitan bishop") of the province. The [delimitation](/wiki/Electoral_boundary_delimitation "Electoral boundary delimitation") of church provinces in the [Latin Church](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church") is reserved to the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See").

There are exceptions to these rules:

* A few dioceses are not part of any province. Their bishops are [exempt](/wiki/Exemption_in_the_Catholic_Church "Exemption in the Catholic Church") from the authority of a metropolitan archbishop, instead reporting directly to the pope. For example, the bishop of the [Diocese of Oslo](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Oslo "Roman Catholic Diocese of Oslo") in Norway.
* A few archdioceses do not belong to provinces and contain no suffragan dioceses. For example, the [Archdiocese of Strasbourg](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Strasbourg "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Strasbourg") in France is not part of a province and has no diocesan bishops reporting to its archbishop.
* Not all archdioceses are metropolitan sees. Five archdioceses are exempt: [Latin Patriarchate of Jerusalem](/wiki/Latin_Patriarchate_of_Jerusalem "Latin Patriarchate of Jerusalem"); [Metropolitan Archdiocese of Udine](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Udine "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Udine"); [Metropolitan Archdiocese of Saint Boniface](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Saint_Boniface "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Saint Boniface"); [Metropolitan Archdiocese of Izmir](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_%C4%B0zmir "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of İzmir") and [Metropolitan Archdiocese of Corfu–Zakynthos–Kefalonia](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Corfu,_Zakynthos,_and_Cephalonia "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Corfu, Zakynthos, and Cephalonia") its leader reports directly to the pope. Other non-metropolitan archdioceses, in contrast, are not exempt. Their archbishops report directly to metropolitan archbishops.

The authority of a Latin Church metropolitan over the other sees within his province is now very limited. During a [vacancy](/wiki/Sede_vacante "Sede vacante") in a suffragan diocese, the metropolitan names a temporary [diocesan administrator](/wiki/Vicar_capitular "Vicar capitular") if the college of [Consultors](/wiki/Consultor "Consultor") of the diocese fails to elect one within the prescribed period.[[4]](#cite_note-4) A metropolitan generally presides at the installation and consecration of a new bishop in the province. The tribunal of the metropolitan see generally serves as the first court of appeal regarding canonical matters of provincial diocesan tribunals. The metropolitan's insignia is the pallium. The article in the *[Catholic Encyclopedia](/wiki/Catholic_Encyclopedia "Catholic Encyclopedia")* of 1911 on *metropolitan* shows that the metropolitan then had scarcely any more power than now.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

In the [Eastern Catholic Churches](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Catholic Churches"), the [patriarchal](/wiki/Patriarch "Patriarch") or [major archiepiscopal](/wiki/Major_archbishop "Major archbishop") Churches may also be divided into ecclesial provinces, each headed by a metropolitan. The [Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church](/wiki/Ukrainian_Greek_Catholic_Church "Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church") has [several](/wiki/Ukrainian_Greek_Catholic_Church#Current_administrative_division "Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church"), two of them in the United States and Canada. Some other Eastern Catholic Churches of a lower category and generally less populous, are known as [metropolitanates](/wiki/Sui_iuris#metropolitan_churches "Sui iuris"). They are headed by a single metropolitan, the hierarch of a fixed [episcopal see](/wiki/Episcopal_see "Episcopal see"),[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) As head of an autonomous Church, his name is mentioned in the [liturgy](/wiki/Liturgy "Liturgy") of that Church immediately after that of the [Pope](/wiki/Pope "Pope") and, in suffragan eparchies, ahead of that of the local hierarch.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

#### Provincial boundary lines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ecclesiastical_province&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Provincial boundary lines")]

The borders of provinces have often been inspired, or even determined, by historical or present political [borders](/wiki/Border "Border"); the same is often true of diocesan borders within a province. The following are some examples:

* In France, where the boundaries partly reflected later Roman provinces, most were rearranged in 2002 to fit new administrative regions.
* A comparable process to that of France occurred earlier in Spain.
* In southern Germany, the diocesan boundaries follow the political boundaries that existed between 1815 and 1945.
* In Ireland, the [four ecclesiastical provinces](/wiki/List_of_Roman_Catholic_dioceses_in_Ireland "List of Roman Catholic dioceses in Ireland") fixed by the [Synod of Kells](/wiki/Synod_of_Kells "Synod of Kells") in 1152 reflected the contemporary boundaries of the [secular provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Ireland "Provinces of Ireland"), but the ecclesiastical provinces and dioceses do not coincide with the present civil province and [county](/wiki/Counties_of_Ireland "Counties of Ireland") borders. Since the [Partition of Ireland](/wiki/Partition_of_Ireland "Partition of Ireland") in 1920–1922 six dioceses in the [province of Armagh](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Armagh "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Armagh") straddle [the international border](/wiki/Irish_border "Irish border") between the [Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland") and [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland"), which is part of the United Kingdom.
* In Scotland, the dioceses, and subsequently the two provinces, follow both civil and geographical boundaries such as rivers.
* In geographically large nations with a sizeable Catholic population, such as the United States, ecclesiastical provinces typically follow state [lines](/wiki/Border "Border"), with less populous states being grouped into provinces. In the United States, there are five exceptions:
  + California has two metropolitan archdioceses and provinces: [Los Angeles](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Los_Angeles "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Los Angeles") and [San Francisco](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_San_Francisco "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of San Francisco").
  + Texas has two metropolitan archdioceses and provinces: [Galveston-Houston](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Galveston-Houston "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Galveston-Houston") and [San Antonio](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_San_Antonio "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of San Antonio").
  + Maryland is unusual in that fourteen of its 23 counties belong to dioceses whose see cities are outside Maryland: (1) the nine counties of Maryland's [Eastern Shore](/wiki/Eastern_Shore_of_Maryland "Eastern Shore of Maryland") ([Delmarva Peninsula](/wiki/Delmarva_Peninsula "Delmarva Peninsula")) are part of the [Diocese of Wilmington](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Wilmington "Roman Catholic Diocese of Wilmington"), Delaware, and (2) the five counties adjacent to the District of Columbia and in southern Maryland are part of the [Archdiocese of Washington](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Washington "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Washington"), which is a different province. Only the remaining nine counties and the City of Baltimore are part of the [Archdiocese of Baltimore](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Baltimore "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Baltimore").
  + [Fishers Island](/wiki/Fishers_Island "Fishers Island"), a part of Suffolk County, New York, and north of [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island"), is part of the [Diocese of Norwich](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Norwich "Roman Catholic Diocese of Norwich"), Connecticut, which is in a different province.
  + Those parts of Idaho and Montana that are within [Yellowstone National Park](/wiki/Yellowstone_National_Park "Yellowstone National Park") are part of the [Diocese of Cheyenne](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Cheyenne "Roman Catholic Diocese of Cheyenne"), Wyoming, which is in a different province.

:   In addition, the [Diocese of Gallup](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Gallup "Roman Catholic Diocese of Gallup") (New Mexico) contains two Arizona [counties](/wiki/County_(United_States) "County (United States)")—[Apache County](/wiki/Apache_County "Apache County") and [Navajo County](/wiki/Navajo_County "Navajo County")—and part of a third county, i.e., those parts of the [Navajo](/wiki/Navajo_Nation "Navajo Nation") and [Hopi](/wiki/Hopi_Reservation "Hopi Reservation") [reservations](/wiki/Indian_reservation "Indian reservation") that are in [Coconino County](/wiki/Coconino_County,_Arizona "Coconino County, Arizona") (Arizona). New Mexico and Arizona, however, together form one province.

* Many countries contain more than one province, except those with a small population or few Catholics.
* In at least one case, a province contains dioceses that are in more than one nation, e.g., the Province of Samoa-Apia, of which the metropolitan see (the [Archdiocese of Samoa-Apia](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Samoa-Apia "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Samoa-Apia")) is in the Independent State of Samoa, and its only suffragan see (the [Diocese of Samoa-Pago Pago](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Samoa-Pago_Pago "Roman Catholic Diocese of Samoa-Pago Pago")) is in American Samoa (an unincorporated territory of the United States). Even individual dioceses, let alone ecclesiastical provinces, can comprise more than one state: examples are [San Marino-Montefeltro](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_San_Marino-Montefeltro "Roman Catholic Diocese of San Marino-Montefeltro") ([San Marino](/wiki/San_Marino "San Marino") and part of Italy), [Urgell](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Urgell "Roman Catholic Diocese of Urgell") ([Andorra](/wiki/Andorra "Andorra") and part of Spain), and the [Diocese of Rome](/wiki/Diocese_of_Rome "Diocese of Rome") itself ([Vatican City](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City") and part of Italy).

### Eastern Orthodox Church

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ecclesiastical_province&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Eastern Orthodox Church")]

Historical development of ecclesiastical provinces in the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") was influenced by strong tendencies of internal administrative centralization. Since the [First Ecumenical Council](/wiki/First_Ecumenical_Council "First Ecumenical Council") (325), the Archbishop of Alexandria was given supreme jurisdiction over all provinces of Egypt. Similar authority was also granted to Archbishop of Antioch regarding jurisdiction over provinces of Orient. Since the [Fourth Ecumenical Council](/wiki/Fourth_Ecumenical_Council "Fourth Ecumenical Council") (451), [Patriarch of Constantinople](/wiki/Patriarch_of_Constantinople "Patriarch of Constantinople") was given the right to consecrate metropolitan bishops in all regions that were placed under his supreme jurisdiction.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeyendorff1989-9) In time, previous administrative autonomy of original ecclesiastical provinces was gradually and systematically reduced in favor of patriarchal centralization. Ancient practice of annual councils of provincial bishops, headed by their local metropolitans, was also abandoned in favor of centralized councils, headed by patriarchs and attended by metropolitan bishops.

The creation of new autonomous and [autocephalous](/wiki/Autocephaly "Autocephaly") jurisdictions was also marked by tendencies of internal centralization. The newly created [Archbishopric of Ohrid](/wiki/Archbishopric_of_Ohrid "Archbishopric of Ohrid") (1018) was structured as a single ecclesiastical province, headed by an archbishop who had jurisdiction over all of his suffragan bishops. In 1219, autocephalous [Serbian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Serbian_Orthodox_Church "Serbian Orthodox Church") was also organized as one ecclesiastical province, headed by an archbishop with direct jurisdiction over all Serbian bishops.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEĆirković200440-46-10) By the end of [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), each autocephalous and autonomous church in Eastern Orthodoxy was functioning as a single, internally integrated ecclesiastical province, headed by a local patriarch or archbishop.

Only in modern times, some Eastern Orthodox Churches have revived the ancient practice by creating internal ecclesiastical provinces on the middle (regional) level of church administration. In the [Romanian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Romanian_Orthodox_Church "Romanian Orthodox Church") there are six regional metropolitanates, headed by local metropolitans who preside over regional synods of local bishops, and have special duties and privileges. For example, the [Metropolitan of Oltenia](/wiki/Metropolis_of_Oltenia "Metropolis of Oltenia") has regional jurisdiction over four local dioceses. On the other hand, a majority of Eastern Orthodox Churches remain and function as highly centralized church bodies, each of them functioning as a single ecclesiastical province.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKiminas2009-11)

### Protestantism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ecclesiastical_province&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Protestantism")]

#### Anglican Communion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ecclesiastical_province&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Anglican Communion")]

Further information: [Anglican Communion § Provinces](/wiki/Anglican_Communion#Provinces "Anglican Communion")

Member churches of the [Anglican Communion](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion") are often referred to as **provinces**. Some provinces are coterminous with the boundaries of political states, some include multiple nations while others include only parts of a nation. Some, such as the [Church of the Province of West Africa](/wiki/Church_of_the_Province_of_West_Africa "Church of the Province of West Africa"), have the word "province" in their names. These member churches are known as "provinces of the Anglican Communion", and are headed by a [primate](/wiki/Primate_(bishop) "Primate (bishop)"), who is usually also styled archbishop, but may have an alternative title such as primus (for example, the [Primus of the Scottish Episcopal Church](/wiki/Primus_of_the_Scottish_Episcopal_Church "Primus of the Scottish Episcopal Church")), [presiding bishop](/wiki/Presiding_bishop "Presiding bishop"), or [moderator](/wiki/Moderator_of_the_General_Assembly "Moderator of the General Assembly").

The word is also used to refer to a grouping of [dioceses](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese") within a member church, commonly known as a metropolitical province, metropolitan province, or internal province. The [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") is divided into two such provinces: [Canterbury](/wiki/Province_of_Canterbury "Province of Canterbury") and [York](/wiki/Province_of_York "Province of York"). The [Anglican Church of Australia](/wiki/Anglican_Church_of_Australia "Anglican Church of Australia") has five provinces: [New South Wales](/wiki/Province_of_New_South_Wales "Province of New South Wales"), [Queensland](/wiki/Province_of_Queensland "Province of Queensland"), [South Australia](/wiki/Anglican_Province_of_South_Australia "Anglican Province of South Australia"), [Victoria](/wiki/Province_of_Victoria "Province of Victoria") and [Western Australia](/wiki/Province_of_Western_Australia "Province of Western Australia"), and an extraprovincial diocese of [Tasmania](/wiki/Anglican_Diocese_of_Tasmania "Anglican Diocese of Tasmania"). The [Anglican Church of Canada](/wiki/Anglican_Church_of_Canada "Anglican Church of Canada") has four: [British Columbia and Yukon](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_Province_of_British_Columbia_and_Yukon "Ecclesiastical Province of British Columbia and Yukon"), [Canada](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_Province_of_Canada "Ecclesiastical Province of Canada"), [Ontario](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_Province_of_Ontario "Ecclesiastical Province of Ontario"), and the [Northern Lights](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_Province_of_the_Northern_Lights "Ecclesiastical Province of the Northern Lights"). The [Church of Ireland](/wiki/Church_of_Ireland "Church of Ireland") has two: [Armagh](/wiki/Province_of_Armagh_(Church_of_Ireland) "Province of Armagh (Church of Ireland)") and [Dublin](/wiki/Province_of_Dublin_(Church_of_Ireland) "Province of Dublin (Church of Ireland)"). The [Episcopal Church in the United States of America](/wiki/Episcopal_Church_in_the_United_States_of_America "Episcopal Church in the United States of America") (ECUSA) numbers, rather than names, its [nine provinces](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_Provinces_and_Dioceses_of_the_Episcopal_Church "Ecclesiastical Provinces and Dioceses of the Episcopal Church"). In all cases apart from ECUSA each metropolitan or internal province is headed by a [metropolitan bishop](/wiki/Metropolitan_bishop "Metropolitan bishop") with the title archbishop.

#### Evangelical State Church in Prussia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ecclesiastical_province&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Evangelical State Church in Prussia")]

The *Evangelical State Church in Prussia*, formed in 1821 (renamed: *Evangelical State Church in Prussia's older Provinces* in 1875, [Evangelical Church of the old-Prussian Union](/wiki/Evangelical_Church_of_the_old-Prussian_Union "Evangelical Church of the old-Prussian Union") in 1922), had ecclesiastical provinces (Kirchenprovinzen) as administrative subsections mostly following the boundaries of those political [Provinces of Prussia](/wiki/Provinces_of_Prussia "Provinces of Prussia") which formed part of the state before 1866, with some border changes after 1920 following WWI territorial cessions.

## Religious institutes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ecclesiastical_province&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Religious institutes")]

The term *province*, or occasionally **religious province**, also refers to a geographical and administrative subdivision in a number of [orders](/wiki/Catholic_religious_order "Catholic religious order") and [congregations](/wiki/Religious_congregation "Religious congregation"). This is true of most, though not all, religious communities founded after the year AD 1000, as well as the [Augustinians](/wiki/Augustinians "Augustinians"), who date from earlier.

A province of a religious institute is typically headed by a [provincial superior](/wiki/Provincial_superior "Provincial superior"). The title differs by each institute's tradition (provincial minister for [Franciscans](/wiki/Franciscan "Franciscan"); provincial prior for [Dominicans](/wiki/Dominican_Order "Dominican Order"); provincial for the Augustinians, simply "provincial" or "provincial father" for the [Jesuits](/wiki/Jesuit "Jesuit") and many others, for instance).

The borders of a religious institute's provinces are determined independently of any diocesan structure, and so the borders often differ from the 'secular', or diocesan, ecclesiastical provinces. The orders' provinces are usually far larger than a diocese, a secular province, or even a country, though sometimes they are smaller in an institute's [heartland](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/heartland "wikt:heartland").

Most [monastic orders](/wiki/Monastic_order "Monastic order") are not organized by provinces. In general, they organise their administration through autonomous houses, in some cases grouped in larger families. For example, each [Benedictine](/wiki/Benedictine "Benedictine") [abbey](/wiki/Abbey "Abbey") is an independent foundation, but will often choose to group themselves into [congregations](/wiki/Congregation_(group_of_houses) "Congregation (group of houses)") based on historical connections.
